Amazon's Core Products and Services

Amazon offers a diverse array of products and services across multiple sectors, positioning itself as a leader in both the retail and technology industries. The company's core offerings include retail sales through online and physical stores, which serve customers in North America as well as internationally. Amazon's retail operations are supported by its extensive range of subscription services, such as Amazon Prime, which provides members with exclusive benefits. Additionally, Amazon is a major player in the technology space through Amazon Web Services, which delivers cloud computing, storage, database, and machine learning services. The company also manufactures and sells a wide range of electronic devices, such as its Kindle e-readers, Fire tablets, Echo smart speakers, and Ring security systems. Amazon's offerings also extend beyond retail and technology with a wide range of creators and sellers with platforms that allow them to publish and sell products, including books, music, and digital content.

Amazon Fun Facts

How many products does Amazon have?

The number of products available on Amazon is constantly changing as new listings are always being added across various categories.

How many distribution centers are in Amazon's supply chain?

Amazon's fulfillment network includes hundreds of facilities around the world, including sortable and non-sortable fulfillment centers, sortation centers, receive centers, and delivery stations, according to the company.

How many countries is Amazon available in?

Products from Amazon are available to customers in more than 100 countries and regions, according to Amazon.

Conclusion

Amazon's meteoric rise from an online bookstore to a global powerhouse is a testament to its relentless innovation and expansion across industries. With its comprehensive product offerings, ranging from consumer goods and electronics to cloud computing and digital content, Amazon continues to redefine the boundaries of retail and technology. The company's vast operations, supported by its three key segments, North America, International, and Amazon Web Services, ensure a dominant presence in global markets. As Amazon continues to evolve, catering to millions of consumers, businesses, and creators worldwide, its influence shows no signs of waning. Investors who have or are considering a position in Amazon can find confidence in the company's ongoing growth and leadership in the eCommerce, cloud, and digital content spaces.

data.title: Still Flying 'VRP Air' After The Distribution Increase
data.description:
I've been long on the Invesco Variable Rate Preferred ETF (NYSEARCA:VRP) since 2022 and have been (generally) increasing the size of my position over the past two-and-a-half years. Just to clarify, this fund holds Preferred Stock in a variety of companies, but mostly from the Financials sector. Furthermore, the securities it owns are variable (~floating) rate, meaning that the dividend payments into the fund will reset periodically.

Deep research was lacking (unfortunately) in my initial purchase decision. In early 2022, amid signs that inflation was starting to pick up, I didn't want to own traditional fixed income securities, and I figured that a variable rate ETF would serve me well. Looking back to that time, after more than a decade of ultra-low inflation, there remained a lot of doubters who questioned whether inflation would actually meaningfully rise again. Investors who saw the signals and positioned themselves correctly for rising inflation did well. I can personally claim the former, but less so the latter.

I got into VRP too early. It's taken quite a while for my investment thesis to play out and for VRP to deliver the returns I was expecting. With that said, robust performance has continued this year even despite declining Treasury yields.

I first wrote about VRP three months ago in July 2024, more than two years after I first bought shares of VRP. This past summer, despite impending FOMC rate cuts, I expected VRP's distributions to rise during the second half of 2024. That has just transpired in an sudden leap, as Invesco announced an October distribution of $0.1428, more than 30% higher than the payout in September.

Today, I want to analyze what this means for the ETF going forward.

VRP Holdings and Credit Exposure

With a NAV of about $1.80 billion, VRP is much smaller than better-known Preferred Share ETFs like the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF), the Invesco Preferred ETF (PGX), the First Trust Preferred Securities and Income ETF (FPE) and others. I find this fact quite fascinating, actually. It leaves me wondering whether retail investors are simply reluctant to manage their interest rate risk, or whether they do it through other means. As a public service announcement to investors, there are a lot of non-traditional investment vehicles readily available to assist in the management of your portfolio exposures - if you search for them, you will find them.

As mentioned above, VRP is quite heavily weighted in the Financial sector, as are most Preferred Stock ETFs. There's a simple reason for this - financial companies are the heaviest issuers of preferred shares. In fact, many bellwether non-financial companies have no preferred stock at all. That doesn't make diversification impossible, and the VanEck Preferred Securities ex-Financials ETF (PFXF) is unique in offering an entire ETF with no financial sector exposure, although it's not of the variable rate variety.

The issuers carried in VRP strangle the investment grade line, with 58% rated investment grade by Standard & Poor's (SPGI) and 61% by Moody's (MCO). A good chunk are rated just below the investment grade level. So the fund isn't without credit risk, and the bank-run fears amidst the failure of Silicon Valley Bank in March 2023 led me to, temporarily, lessen my exposure to VRP. In general, though, I'm not preoccupied by solvency risk through VRP, and investors should remember that preferred shareholders have priority over common shareholders in the event of liquidation or restructuring.

Rate Exposure

Interest Rate exposure is what sets VRP apart from peers. The easiest way to illustrate that is by comparing it to Invesco's own non-variable Preferred ETF that trades under the ticker PGX.

Now, I'm a little removed from my CFA studies, but I'm a bit shocked by the significant gap between PGX's modified duration and effective duration. Morningstar has the numbers a little closer than Invesco's own duration reporting, but let's default to assuming something weird has happened with Effective Duration over the study period (and it might be related to financial sector credit concerns and recovery).

On a Modified Duration (the academic measure) basis, PGX has a duration 3.5x higher (15.11 versus 3.35) than VRP. In layman terms, this means that VRP carries substantially less interest rate risk, and that can be explained by the resetting feature of the variable rate preferred shares that VRP holds.

For those following closely, my initial mistake in 2022 was assuming that VRP's duration was actually much lower. For some reason, I was anticipating ~semi-annual resets, and while short-term resets are applicable to some of VRP's holdings, it's not the standard, on average.

As can be seen from the below total return chart, VRP, which I expected to outperform as interest rates started rising in mid-2022, didn't experience a notable outperformance (over vanilla preferred share ETFs) until 2023 got rolling. There was quite a lag here.

Since the beginning of 2023 it's been a much different story. I'm not entirely sure the market has been completely efficient in its pricing of Preferred Securities (duration variances should trigger immediate pricing differences) but over time VRP has delivered better performance, as originally expected.

VRP In The Face Of Fed Rate Cuts

Here's where things get a bit more slippery. Since the U.S. Federal Reserve started their recent interest rate cut cycle, variable-rate VRP has actually outperformed vanilla preferred stock ETFs. How can this be?

Well, first of all, the Feds Funds rate represents only the very short end of the Treasury curve, and most preferred shares, including those with variable rates, don't trade at the very short end. The more developed explanation is that, despite the Fed cut rates, 2-to-10 year yields have risen by about 50 bps across the curve over the past month (Higher yields = lower prices for longer-duration securities).

I believe there's a 3rd reason here, which is that the market has probably been inefficient in pricing-in preferred stock dividend resets, as hard as that is to believe. What's important for readers to understand is that even if yields are decreasing, it doesn't mean that the securities that VRP owns will reset lower.

Reset Frequencies

In the case of some (not all) securities held by VRP, there's a multi-year lag in the resetting of the variable dividend rate. For instance, a fixed-to-floating rate preferred stock issued in 2021 that resets for the first time this month would almost certainly deliver a higher dividend rate than it was previously paying.

This helps explain how VRP could declare (as it did) its largest monthly distribution of 2024 in October, a month after a 50bps Fed Funds Rate cut.

Other preferred securities owned by VRP, however, incur more frequent resets. Consider, for example, the 2 preferred securities the ETF owns from Synovus Corp:

The top (blue) Synovus Series D Preferred yields a floating quarterly rate. When the rate resets on December 21, it will likely pay about 1% less than it had been yielding recently (due to two upcoming 25bps expected Fed Fund Rate cuts atop the 50bps cut that occurred in September).

On the other hand, the (orange) Synovus Series E Preferred resets only every 5 years, and did so recently on July 1, 2024. This means that this security will continue to pay its current dividend rate (which I estimate at ~8.50%) until July 2029, regardless of what the Federal Reserve does between now and then. That's great for holders of VRP, and not great at all for Synovus as we enter a rate cut cycle!

Without labouring through the details of every single holding to assess when it resets, we can lean on the duration measure. Morningstar is reporting a modified duration measure of 2.97 for VRP, versus 3.35 as reported by Invesco itself. Let's split the difference and assume that VRP's interest rate sensitivity is 3.16 (years). That means the (weighted) average rate reset for holdings within VRP is about 38 months.

VRP's Upcoming Resets

In studying the details of the ETF's preferred stock holdings, I've found that nearly 20% of the securities held should incur resets prior to the end of Q1 2023. See below.

Anticipated Reset % of Holdings
before the end of 2024 12.53%
Q1 2025 7.03%
remainder of 2025 10.58%
2026+ 69.73%

It's important to remember that only some of these resets will be favourable for VRP. Any preferred security that has been resetting quarterly, semi-annually, or even annually will likely begin to take on a lower dividend rate, while those that reset every several years, may see their coupon rate increase (and possibly substantially so).

On average though, I expect VRP's weighted average coupon rate to continue to rise into 2025, regardless of future rate cuts, and the ETF's duration value is supportive of this.

Value and Conclusion

VRP's recent (October 2024) monthly distribution rate of $0.1428 equates to an annualized yield of ~7.0% at the ETF's recent unit price. That's higher than I expected when I wrote in July. VRP classifies the entire $0.1428 as ordinary income, meaning that there are no capital gains included here.

However, incoming dividend cashflows (to VRP) can vary from month to month. Looking at the historical distribution chart leaves one wondering if most of VRP's dividend receipts are heavily weighted towards the end of the calendar year.

I don't believe that VRP's recent ~$0.143 monthly distribution rate is sustainable throughout the upcoming year. However, I do believe that the ETF can pay out higher distributions than it has been over the past 6 months ($0.1146 on average).

Putting it all together - the reset mechanisms, VRP's duration, and Fed Funds rate expectations - I'm forecasting an average distribution rate of $0.125 for VRP over the next 6 months, which would translate into an annualized yield of about 6.15%.

I'm still a holder of VRP on that basis. There also might be a short-term opportunity for new investors to capture good yield on VRP over the next several months. However, I'm likely to adopt a more cautious stance on VRP as we enter 2025, all dependent on what happens between now and then, of course.

Risks

The major risk investors in VRP need to continuously be mindful of is the heavy concentration in Financial sector preferred shares. Any sort of financial/credit crisis that results in solvency concerns for banks could trigger significant losses in the ETF. In such a scenario, however, the losses would likely be much less than for those holding common stock in the affected firms.

data.title: Viasat Earnings Preview: When Cheap Isn't Enough
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Viasat, Inc. (NASDAQ:VSAT) is set to report its fiscal Q2 2025 in approximately 2 weeks' time. With its stock moving towards its decade low, investors are undoubtedly hoping for a relief rally and an update to its guidance.

For my part, as you'll soon see, I remain thoroughly unconvinced over its prospects.

Indeed, I argue that even though this stock “seems” cheap at 1x EBITDA, that its EBITDA isn't worth much. More specifically, I do not see a clear path in the near term for Viasat to bring its debt levels under control.

In sum, I believe that in the coming months, investors will look back to $11 per share as a price to aspire towards.

Rapid Recap

Back in May, I said,

In short, the stock looks cheap, particularly as it's close to its all-time lows. But it's I charge that it's more likely than not that it will continue to get even cheaper from this point forward.

As you can see above, this highly shorted stock has been a gut-wrenching experience for both the longs and shorts in this name. With a dramatic blow for the shorts a few months ago, many would have believed that I had made a wrong call. Indeed, several readers reached out to me to say this much.

Yet, I remain adamant, that this stock is a sell. Now, to be clear, a sell does not mean I recommend shorting a stock, a sell simply means that I believe there are better investment opportunities elsewhere.

Viasat's Near-Term Prospects

Viasat provides satellite communication services, which allow people and businesses to stay connected, whether on airplanes, at sea, or in remote areas. They launch satellites to provide high-speed internet and secure communications, especially for governments.

Viasat delivers connectivity through its global satellite network. Customers, ranging from airlines to defense agencies, benefit from secure high-speed internet. By integrating its recently acquired Inmarsat assets, Viasat enhances its satellite services.

Meanwhile, it must be noted that Viasat remains optimistic about its future growth, driven by strong performance in aviation and defense.

Furthermore, during the previous earnings call, Viasat noted that its capex is expected to decline over the next couple of years, leading the path to positive free cash flows in the coming year (to be expounded upon soon).

Nevertheless, note that Viasat operates in a highly competitive communications sector, where some of its competitors, like Astranis (PRIVATE), are exploring new strategies, such as using smaller satellites.

Given this background, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Are Unimpressive

In my previous analysis, I stated,

Recall that Viasat acquired Inmarsat just over halfway through fiscal Q1 2024. This means that fiscal Q1 2025 will still have a few weeks of inorganic growth to report, but once we are past these few weeks and into fiscal Q2 2025, Viasat will have to compare with the very high hurdles of the prior year.

My bear case for Viasat isn't to be found in its growth rates. Even if Viasat upwards revises its guidance for fiscal 2026, I'm not sure if this will be enough to distract investors away from its clear bear thesis, that we discuss next.

VSAT Stock Valuation? 1x EBITDA is Not Cheap Enough

I'm an Inflection investor. For me, the starting point for any valuation is the company's balance sheet. I want to know whether the balance sheet is going to contribute or detract from my investment thesis. I'm not a believer in overcomplicating investing. On this front, consider the following.

This is a business that is over-leveraged. Pure and simple. Yes, there's a valuation where it can make sense to back an over-leveraged business. But I'm not convinced that VSAT is trading cheaply enough to compensate me for this financial position.

And then, moreover, consider this line from the shareholder letter:

We are making steady progress and consistent with prior guidance, we continue to expect an inflection to positive free cash flow by the end of Q1 FY2026.

This means that until next year, the business will not be free cash flow positive. What sort of business carries more debt than its market cap and is not going to generate any free cash flow for yet another twelve months?

Indeed, if the business is capable of making about $1.7 billion of EBITDA this year, the stock is priced at 1x EBITDA, but is that truly a bargain? I'm utterly unconvinced.

The Bottom Line

After reviewing Viasat's debt profile and lackluster prospects, I see no compelling reason to invest in the stock.

Despite it trading at 1x EBITDA, its heavily leveraged balance sheet and the prolonged timeline to positive free cash flow make it a risky bet.

This is a case where “cheap” doesn’t mean undervalued.

Ultimately, it seems Viasat has dug itself into a hole that will take time to climb out of—too long for my liking. I'll pass on this one; it's not worth the risk. Sometimes, trying to reach for the stars lands you in hot water.

data.title: SolarEdge: Core European Market Continues To Deteriorate - Strong Sell (Downgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have covered SolarEdge Technologies, Inc. (NASDAQ:SEDG) or “SolarEdge” previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Since my last update on leading solar inverter system supplier SolarEdge Technologies, the company's share price has deteriorated to new multi-year lows:

Recently, numerous analysts have become increasingly cautious about near-term prospects for SolarEdge's core European market.

According to TD Cowen, Q3 residential installations in Germany, one of the company's largest markets, were down 13% on a sequential basis and 29% year-over-year due to several issues, including:

  • Customers have become less concerned about rising energy prices.
  • Weak economic conditions have resulted in reduced customer confidence levels and declining purchasing power.

In addition, other leading European markets are experiencing headwinds from regulatory uncertainties (Netherlands) and anticipated lower utility rates (France).

Moreover, with Chinese competitors aggressively trying to take market share, pricing pressure remains a concern.

After the close of Tuesday's session, competitor Enphase Energy, Inc. (ENPH) or “Enphase” reported disappointing third quarter results and provided very weak fourth quarter guidance.

While Enphase's sales in Europe were down 15% sequentially, sell-through of the company's products was down an eye-catching 34% thus resulting in the requirement to further reduce shipments into the European channel.

Even worse, Enphase management expects things in Europe to get even worse in the near term.

However, Enphase derives only 25% of its revenues from sources outside the United States, as compared to 75% for SolarEdge. In FY2023, Europe represented approximately 64% of the company's sales.

In addition, Enphase has continued to generate substantial free cash flow in recent quarters, while SolarEdge has burned $750 million since the beginning of 2023.

Following a $300 million convertible notes offering in June, the company finished the second quarter with $810.1 million in cash and liquid investments and $647.5 million in debt. After quarter-end, the company issued another $37 million in 2029 convertible notes due to initial purchasers exercising the overallotment option.

However, with negative gross margins, high inventory levels and a weaker-than-expected operating environment in Europe, cash burn is likely to continue for at least the next couple of quarters which would be in contrast to management's previously stated expectations.

Remember also that SolarEdge will have to redeem $347.5 million in convertible notes in less than twelve months. Assuming a quarterly cash burn of around $135 million, the company would end Q3/2025 with cash and liquid investments below $100 million, implying a cash runway of about a year.

In addition, with quarterly revenues down and inventories up by more than 70% year-over-year, I would expect SolarEdge's Q3 results to include a sizeable inventory charge.

Please note also that following the abrupt resignation of the company's long-standing CEO Zvi Lando two months ago, SolarEdge still hasn't installed new permanent leadership.

However, with the company's core markets deteriorating even further, decisive action will be required. SolarEdge needs to significantly reduce operating expenses, improve gross margins and likely raise additional capital to ensure survival beyond next year.

Given these issues, I am downgrading SolarEdge's shares from “Sell” to “Strong Sell” going into the company's Q3 report on November 6.

Bottom Line

With SolarEdge Technologies' core market expected to deteriorate even further going into 2025, interim management will likely be required to take decisive action.

For my part, I wouldn't be surprised to see the company announce substantial headcount reductions, facility closures and material inventory charges when the company reports Q3 results in early November. Even another capital raise might be in the cards.

Given these issues, I am downgrading SolarEdge Technologies' shares from “Sell” to “Strong Sell” going into the company's Q3 report on November 6.

Risk Factors

While highly unlikely, a better-than-feared Q3 report and outlook would likely result in a major relief rally in SolarEdge Technologies, Inc.'s shares.

However, competitor Enphase Energy's disappointing third quarter results and outlook have made it apparent that there won't be an easy cure to SolarEdge's persistent issues anytime soon.

data.title: JAKKS Pacific: Assessing The Stock This Holiday Season And Beyond
data.description:
Toy company JAKKS Pacific (NASDAQ:JAKK) is scheduled to next report earnings post-market on Oct. 30. As you may recall, shares soared immediately after the last quarterly earnings release on Aug. 1, thanks to the announcement of better-than-feared results and outlook.

JAKK stock has since continued to climb higher, on the expectation that a spate of upcoming family film releases will translate into a rebound in content-related toy sales for the company. Next week's quarterly earnings release will provide further indication whether or not this bullish narrative will play out.

However, assuming that JAKKS Pacific once again reports well-received results and updates to guidance, to what extent will this translate into higher prices for shares? Despite JAKK's low valuation, upside potential may not be as significant as it seems at first glance.

Background and Recent Developments

Founded in 1995, JAKKS Pacific has managed to survive as a smaller competitor to peers like Hasbro (HAS) and Mattel (MAT), largely by focusing its efforts on toy products based on intellectual property licensed from Disney (DIS) and other media content companies.

JAKKS does have several proprietary brands offering evergreen toy products. The company has a Halloween costume division, Disguise Inc., as well, content-related toy products remain the company's bread-and-butter.

Historically, the flip-side to this heavy focus on content-based toys has been earnings volatility. As seen below, JAKKS Pacific experienced wild swings in revenue and profitability during the 2010s. In turn, leading to poor stock price performance during this time frame as well.

However, since 2020, operating performance has steadily improved, thanks to two factors. First, as discussed in JAKKS Pacific's most recent investor presentation, evergreen product lines have become an increasingly larger portion of the company's overall business.

Secondly, during this time frame, the company has returned to consistent profitability. After several years of negative operating margins, operating margins climbed back into the low single-digits in 2020, and during 2021, 2022, and 2023, steadily climbed up high single-digits.

In November 2019, JAKKS Pacific appointed John Kimble as CFO. Kimble, a veteran exec who previously held management roles at Disney and Mattel, is likely the key reason behind this improvement in operational performance.

As a result of improving circumstances, JAKK stock has surged steadily higher over the past five years, albeit with more choppy price performance as of late.

This can be chalked up largely to a weakening in fiscal performance earlier this year, but this weakening could prove temporary. Driven largely by a lack of hit family movies, which in turn led to a lack of hit toys based on licensed content, this is likely to reverse in the latter months of this year.

As Seeking Alpha commentator Caffital Research pointed out back in July, toy products related to two upcoming film releases, Moana 2 and Sonic the Hedgehog 3, are expected to drive a "content-led" recovery in Q4 and beyond.

Again though, it's questionable how much impact upcoming results and guidance may have on the price of JAKK stock, as the rebound may already be factored into the valuation of shares.

Why a Content-Related Rebound May Prove to be Moderate at Best

Q3, or the quarter ending Sep. 30, is the quarter in which retailers both lock down costume inventory for Halloween, and toy inventory for the Christmas shopping season. Hence, a disproportionate amount of JAKKS Pacific's overall sales and earnings each year occur during this period.

Per sell-side consensus, JAKKS Pacific is expected to report GAAP earnings of $3.85 per share during Q3 2024. With these high earnings helping to wipe out losses incurred during other quarters, hitting this target will point to the company reporting full-year earnings per share (EPS) of $3.14. In 2025, as new family film releases continue to positively affect content-related toy demand, forecasts call for JAKK's annual earnings to bounce back further, up to $3.79 per share.

At first glance, it may appear that the market has yet to factor this earnings bonanza into the JAKK stock price. After all, based on these forecasts, JAKK currently trades for just 8.05 times estimated 2024 earnings, and 6.67 times estimated 2025 earnings.

Compare this to the valuation of JAKKS Pacific's key publicly traded peers. Hasbro trades for 18.6 times estimated 2024 earnings, and 16.07 times estimated 2025 earnings. Mattel trades for 12.6 times estimated 2024 earnings, 11.47 times estimated 2025 earnings.

Still, while strong content-led growth could help shares surge back to their near-term high-water mark in the mid-$30s, to fully bridge the valuation gap between JAKK stock and its peers, more permanent changes may need to occur at the company.

Namely, a further reduction in reliance on the content-licensing business. This could be achieved through both organic growth, as well as acquisition.

JAKK's Best Path to Higher Prices

A key differentiator between JAKKS Pacific and competitors like Hasbro and Mattel is, while JAKKS has focused on licensing intellectual property from third-party owners of media franchises, Hasbro and Mattel have in recent years have focused on either acquiring media franchises and/or expanding into the production and distribution of content based on their respective franchises.

Based on their respective valuations, the market clearly appreciates the latter rather than the former. Admittedly, JAKKS has its work cut out for it, when it comes to building up a media franchise portfolio on par with Hasbro and Mattel.

However, the company has been making moves in the right direction. A key example is with JAKK's proprietary Wild Manes product line. Beyond developing the toy line, JAKKS Pacific has also developed an animated series and Roblox (RBLX) game based on the franchise as well.

Only time will tell whether Wild Manes becomes the next My Little Pony, but even a modicum of success could have a big impact on JAKK's bottom line. Besides new product/franchise lines developed organically, JAKKS could also pursue opportunities via acquisition.

The company may have had just $17.7 million in cash on hand as of June 30, 2024, but in the coming quarters, the aforementioned rebound in content-related toy sales stands to produce significant operating cash flow, helping to build back up this cash position.

JAKKS Pacific could also boost cash flow/its cash position through further cost reduction efforts, especially when it comes to corporate-level expenses. For example, JAKKS leases around 65,858 square feet of headquarters and showroom space in pricey Santa Monica, California. Corporate office space in this area leases for between $40 and $60 per square foot annually.

Although it makes sense that a content-focused toy company would need office space in a swanky section of L.A., eventually moving the HQ closer to the company's main distribution center in admittedly less glamorous City of Industry, California, could lead to cost savings, with little impact on the company's ability to maintain its licensing relationships with Disney and other major media companies.

Executive compensation, particularly for CEO Stephen G. Berman, appears excessive in relation to the overall size of JAKKS Pacific. Executive compensation for Berman totaled $10.5 million in 2023, and $13.1 million in 2022.

Yes, it'll likely take something like shareholder activism to successfully push for Berman's removal. There would also be the one-time impact of "golden parachute" payments made to Berman under a "change of control" scenario. Still, this is yet another example of how much profitability could be improved through cost reduction efforts.

$50+ Per Share Within Reach If a Further Evergreen Pivot Takes Shape

A further pivot to evergreen toys, coupled with other possible operational improvement efforts, would likely go a long way in pushing JAKK stock to significantly higher prices.

For instance, if such efforts enabled JAKKS Pacific to consistently generate earnings in line with current forecasts, this could be enough to drive a re-rating to a low-teens forward earnings multiple on par with MAT.

Applying a 12.7x multiple to JAKK's 2025 forecasted EPS of $3.79 gives us a valuation of around $47.75 per share. Add in the potential impact of cost-cutting on the bottom line, and it's easy to see how shares could climb to prices far north of $50 per share, if a further evergreen pivot takes shape.

Risks and Bottom Line

It goes without saying that my bull case for JAKK stock is highly speculative at this point. Although the company has been highlighting its success in evergreen toy products, it's not as if JAKKS Pacific has decided to begin aggressively moving away from its dependence on licensed intellectual property.

JAKKS Pacific's small size may also limit its ability to scale up, whether organically or through bolt-on acquisitions. In fact, JAKKS Pacific may be a better takeover target than an acquirer. For instance, a toy company like Spin Master (OTCPK:SNMSF), the company behind children's media and toy franchise Paw Patrol, could see the potential to wring out cost synergies. That said, discussion about JAKK's potential as a takeover target is also highly speculative at this point.

To top things off, it's hardly a given that, next week, JAKKS impresses the market with strong results and guidance. With expectations already set high, investors could look to any negative aspect to the earnings release as an excuse to sell.

With all of this in mind, you may want to tread carefully ahead of and following the upcoming earnings release. JAKK may need more than a rebound in content-related sales to move the needle.

Unless management makes a further pivot towards evergreen products, or an activist investor emerges to push for major changes at the company, it may be better to wait for the next time JAKK stock falls out of favor with investors before buying.

data.title: AT&T Inc. 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by AT&T Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: We Need To Have A Talk About 'Bond Vigilantes'
data.description:
You just have to love the short-term overreactions of Mr. Market. Just two months ago, we get a weak employment report and investors are panicked that the Fed is too tight. Then we get a strong employment report in September and investors are panicked that the Fed is too loose. In May, the 10-year interest rate was as high as 4.75%. By September, it was as low as 3.6%. Today it’s bounced back to 4.2%. And as rates tick higher in recent months, there’s been a growing chorus about how “bond vigilantes” are going to teach the Fed a lesson. This has been especially loud coming from Stanley Druckenmiller, Paul Tudor Jones and Elon Musk. The basic narrative is that bond markets will teach the Fed and US government a lesson about reckless spending which will drive up interest rates and bankrupt the USA. Except, there’s a huge problem in this narrative - the bond market has a lot less control over interest rates than this story would have you believe.

We should debunk one myth right off the bat. When Musk says the US government is going bankrupt like a household, he’s quite simply misunderstanding how the monetary system works. Individuals go bankrupt. Large aggregated sectors do not. For example, the aggregated private sector cannot go bankrupt. The US government is a huge aggregated sector in the US economy. It does not go bankrupt. In the aggregate, it can print money to fund all spending, and it cannot run out of this money unless it borrows in a foreign currency, which it doesn’t do. Of course, it can cause wildly huge inflation. We know that after Covid, but comparing the Federal government to an individual is just wrong.1

Of course, you might say this is all semantics. High inflation is a form of default. I don’t necessarily disagree! But when we assess the burden of the national debt, we are really assessing the risk of inflation that comes from that debt. So, is the interest on the national debt causing an inflation problem? I think the evidence is very clear on this - no. Just look at the impact of raising interest rates when compared to inflation. If anything, it looks like raising interest rates causes inflation to move down, not up.2

But what about those pesky bond vigilantes? Aren’t they finally going to show up and teach the Fed a lesson? I don’t see it. I don’t even see how it’s possible. For example, the Fed is the reserve monopolist. So, as I’ve explained in the past, they have absolute control over something like short-term interest rates. The market quite literally cannot force them to change this rate because the market cannot compete with the Fed. If a bank tries to set the short-term interest rate, the Fed just comes in and smashes them with their bottomless pit of money. Banks know this, and so they do not fight the Fed.

Of course, this is not true at the long end of the curve because the Fed lets the long end float. As I’ve explained before, the best way to understand the yield curve is to think of the Fed as a dog walker who has absolute control of the leash at the handle and allows the dog to wander further out the leash. The Fed has absolute control over the handle (the Fed Funds Rate) and lets the 30-year wander from side to side, but still within a certain control.

It might look like the dog is walking the Fed, but the Fed always has the ability to pull that leash in and grab that dog by the neck. This is what “yield curve control” would look like, and if the Fed entered the market for 30-year bonds and started explicitly setting a target, they could drive that rate to whatever they wanted. But they let it float a bit. And smart bond traders still know that the 30-year bond is ultimately just a prediction about where the Fed will be on average over the course of 30 years. And that’s where this gets a little more interesting.

If inflation were to surge to 10% again, then the Fed would have to react. What would happen there is the 30-year bond would start to move higher and signal to the Fed that they need to move. It would be similar to a large dog pulling the owner around. The owner has to respond. And the Fed would absolutely respond to a large surge in inflation. So, when inflation is high, you can think of the dog as having more control, and when inflation is low, you can think of the owner as having more control. But the owner always ultimately has control over the dog. It just depends on whether the owner is willing to assert control.

In other words, even if inflation jumped to 10% the Fed could still say “screw it, we’re leaving rates at 0%”. The bond vigilantes could kick and scream all they wanted, but if the Fed entered the market and pegged rates at 0% while inflation was 10%, then rates would fall to 0%. This would be a terrible idea, and it would never happen outside of something like a war-time emergency, but I am just communicating the concept.

Back to Druckenmiller and friends. This seems to be a pretty persistent thing with Druckenmiller. For example, I wrote nearly this exact same article 11 years ago in response to a WSJ op-ed in which Druckenmiller said the exact same thing. He said the USA was bankrupt and that the Fed was too loose. The 10-year yield was 2% at the time, the Fed was on 0% and rates would slowly drift lower until we got the Covid shock 8 years later. It was a dreadful prediction. I think the same exact thing is happening now. Don’t get me wrong. Druckenmiller is one of the all-time great investors, but betting on US government default has been a bad bet for a very long time. In my humble opinion, the error in this analysis is two-fold:

  1. Assuming that interest payments are problematic - They are not, because the Fed can control them with a dial and also because high rates put downward pressure on inflation.
  2. Assuming high inflation must result from government deficits - I think it’s absolutely true that large deficits put upward pressure on inflation. I’ve said this a billion times during Covid. But government spending is 23% of GDP. That’s the same level it was at in 1982! And while it’s a large portion of aggregate spending, we should remember that 77% of spending is coming from other sources. In most cases, it’s much more efficient sources, such as the most efficient corporate machine the human race has ever seen (Corporate America).

Again, don’t get me wrong. When government spending explodes to 45% of GDP like it did in 2021, then big inflation can come from this because the government becomes the primary (reckless) spender in the economy. But that’s not the case today. Government spending as a percentage of GDP is about the same as it’s been for most of the last 40 years. So it begs the question: in an economy like the USA, does the government drive inflation or is the government driving a small amount of inflation that is not as important as other factors in the economy? In my opinion, other factors are driving the secular inflation trend, and that includes:

  1. Huge innovative efficiencies being driven by corporate America that exert downward pressure on inflation.
  2. Demographic changes that are turning America into an older and slower-growing population.
  3. Inequality, which is exerting pressure on those with the highest marginal propensity to spend.

If anything, it takes huge deficits to offset these three huge trends. It takes something like a Covid stimulus to result in big-time inflation. Otherwise, the government, even with its currently large deficits, cannot create high inflation without exploding the deficit again. Will that happen? It’s certainly a risk. But it wouldn’t be my baseline estimate. If anything, the deficit is likely to shrink, in large part because the Fed is going to cut rates in the coming years.

So, long story short - the bond vigilante narrative is overblown. Does it mean we don’t need to worry about government spending and inflation? No, I think investors should continue to protect themselves from inflation, even if it remains low. But we also shouldn’t blow this out of proportion and feed into hyperbolic narratives about how the USA is on the verge of bankruptcy.

______________

1 I sometimes take digs at MMT people for the sloppy aspects of their theory, but this is one thing they’re generally pretty good about communicating. Comparing a household to the federal government is silly.

2 I think it’s useful to be careful about the difference between high inflation and a benign rate of low inflation (as I described here). The USA has prospered in the last 100 years despite persistent 3% inflation. We are by no means “bankrupt”, and in fact, the country is the wealthiest it’s ever been. On the other hand, we also know that very high inflation is a form of default where consumer demand for currency is collapsing, and that is a de facto form of “default”.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
